one_line_takeaway: By re-casting existing single-tool jailbreak prompts as composable encode/decode 'tools' and letting iterative optimizers (TAP, AutoDAN, GCG) search over chains of them — with a contextual-bandit layer reusing experience across attack episodes — JailbreakOPT substantially raises jailbreak success against hardened targets like Claude-Haiku-4.5 and GPT-5.1 (e.g., TAP's ASR on Claude-Haiku-4.5 rises from 0.96% to 56.54%) while often cutting the number of queries needed.
key_finding: On AdvBench (Table 1), tool augmentation substantially improves ASR on the most resistant targets: TAP's ASR rises from 29.46% to 83.46% on GPT-5.1 and from 0.96% to 56.54% on Claude-Haiku-4.5; AutoDAN (LLM-judge)'s ASR rises from 60.19% to 91.15% on GPT-5.1 and from 0.38% to 59.81% on Claude-Haiku-4.5. Tool use also reduces query cost in some settings (e.g., GCG's No.A drops from 64 to 1.57 on Vicuna and from 64 to 1.27 on LLaMA when tool-augmented). No single atomic attack dominates across targets (Flip Attack is strong on GPT-4o [82.31%] and DeepSeek [99.23%] but scores 0% on Claude-Haiku-4.5; Code Chameleon is comparatively robust on Claude [40.77%] and Gemini [63.65%]). In the inter-episode (contextual bandit) study under a reduced 3x8 budget, adding both tool-usage-history and target-model-identity context to TAP raises ASR from a no-context baseline of 19.23%/30.77%/5.77% (GPT-5.1/Gemini/Claude) to 61.54%/98.08%/40.38% respectively, with No.A also generally falling. The LLM judge shows Spearman correlation ρ=0.660 (p<0.0001) with human ratings across 25 responses from 4 PhD annotators, with 38% exact agreement and 63% of ratings within one point. A single-target compute-cost measurement (AutoDAN vs. Claude-Haiku-4.5) reports 41.50 average attack steps, 304.9s wall-clock time, and an estimated $3.38 per complete attack episode.
core_contribution: JailbreakOPT is a tool-assisted framework for stateless, single-turn jailbreak prompt optimization against LLMs. It makes three contributions: (1) a unified abstraction (orchestrator/mutator/evaluator/selector, Algorithm 1) that expresses PAIR, GCG, AutoDAN, and TAP as instances of one iterative search loop; (2) an attack tool library in which each atomic jailbreak technique (e.g., Code Chameleon, Flip Attack, Persuasion) is represented as an encoding/decoding pair, and multiple tools are composed into 'attack chains' via nested encode-decode composition (enc_(a0,a1)=enc_a1∘enc_a0, dec_(a0,a1)=dec_a0∘dec_a1) plus tool-level genetic operators (crossover/mutation over tool sequences, subject to admissibility constraints) so that iterative baselines like TAP/AutoDAN/GCG can select and combine these tools instead of doing raw token/sentence-level mutation; and (3) an inter-episode learning mechanism that models each completed search episode as an 'Action-History-Augmented Decision Process' (AHADP), extracts (context, action, outcome) bandit records from root-to-node trajectories, and trains a contextual (bootstrapped logistic Thompson Sampling) bandit policy to guide future tool selection using tool-usage-history and target-model-identity as context features.
threat_model: Black-box, stateless single-turn jailbreaking: the attacker submits independent standalone prompts to a target LLM (no accumulated multi-turn conversational context) and observes only the model's response, which is scored by an LLM judge (following the PAIR judge protocol, 1-10 scale, threshold-based success) and, in a validation study, by four human PhD annotators. The paper explicitly scopes itself away from stateful multi-turn conversational jailbreaks (e.g., Crescendo), which it treats as a different, non-comparable threat model. Target systems evaluated include open-weight models (Vicuna-13b-v1.5, LLaMA-3-8b, Qwen3-235b-a22b-instruct-2507, DeepSeek-v3.2) and proprietary/hosted models (GPT-4o, GPT-5.1, Gemini-3-Flash, Claude-Haiku-4.5), accessed via a unified API under deterministic decoding (temperature 0.0) for target/judge and temperature 1.0 for attack generation, with 512-token max target generations.
limitations: The paper itself flags: (1) the AutoDAN compute-cost figure is a single-model measurement, not a cross-method cost comparison; (2) GPT-4o/GPT-5.1/Gemini/Claude/Qwen3/DeepSeek results are missing ('–') for AutoDAN(loss) and GCG in Table 1, i.e., those two methods are only evaluated on Vicuna and LLaMA, leaving cross-target comparisons for those baselines incomplete; (3) the inter-episode study only tests two lightweight, hand-chosen context signals (tool usage history, target model identity) and only on TAP as the base optimizer; (4) LLM-judge agreement with humans is only 'substantial' (ρ=0.660, 38% exact match), not near-perfect, so ASR numbers carry residual judge noise; (5) the framework assumes black-box query access with an LLM-based evaluator/judge, and its generality is demonstrated only on the stateless single-turn threat model — the authors explicitly do not claim results transfer to multi-turn conversational jailbreaks.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful instruction prompts, Zou et al. 2023a)
HarmBench (200 examples from standard text-based subset, Mazeika et al. 2024)
quantitative_results: TAP ASR on Claude-Haiku-4.5: 0.96% (w/o tools) -> 56.54% (with tools)
TAP ASR on GPT-5.1: 29.46% (w/o tools) -> 83.46% (with tools)
AutoDAN(LLM-judge) ASR on Claude-Haiku-4.5: 0.38% (w/o tools) -> 59.81% (with tools)
AutoDAN(LLM-judge) ASR on GPT-5.1: 60.19% (w/o tools) -> 91.15% (with tools)
GCG No.A on Vicuna: 64 (w/o tools) -> 1.57 (with tools); on LLaMA: 64 -> 1.27

Inter-episode (TAP, reduced 3x8 budget) ASR with tool-usage-history + target-model-ID context vs no-context baseline: GPT-5.1 19.23%->61.54%, Gemini-3-Flash 30.77%->98.08%, Claude-Haiku-4.5 5.77%->40.38%
LLM-judge vs human agreement: Spearman rho = 0.660 (p<0.0001), 38% exact agreement, 63% within one point, on 25 responses rated by 4 PhD annotators
AutoDAN cost on Claude-Haiku-4.5: 41.50 average attack steps, 304.9s wall-clock, ~$3.38 per attack episode
Flip Attack ASR: 82.31% on GPT-4o, 99.23% on DeepSeek-v3.2, 0% on Claude-Haiku-4.5
Code Chameleon ASR: 40.77% on Claude-Haiku-4.5, 63.65% on Gemini-3-Flash, 93.85% on DeepSeek-v3.2
